segments.s338-g1: و زآن پس is a stacked temporal phrase (و = 'and', زآن = 'from/after that', پس = 'then/after') that sets the time. بخوردند is a past tense plural verb 'they ate' placed before its object (poetic word order); چیزی که بود is a relative phrase 'that which was/whatever there was' functioning as the object, with بود as the past copula inside the relative clause.
segments.s338-g2: ز خوردن is an ablative/causal circumstantial ('from/because of eating'), where خوردن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'eating'. سوی خواب رفتند is literally 'they went toward sleep' (رفتند past plural 'they went'), and زود after the verb qualifies the action as happening soon/quickly.
segments.s338-g3: هم آنگه together form an adverbial 'at that same moment' (هم = 'together/also', آنگه = 'then/thereupon'). برآمد is the past tense 'arose/issued forth', and یکی باد و ابر is an indefinite subject 'a certain wind and cloud' (یکی used as 'a/certain'). Word order (verb before subject) is common in classical verse.
segments.s338-g4: هوا گشت = 'the air became' (گشت = 'became/turned'). بر سان is a compound prepositional simile meaning 'like' or 'in the manner of' (بر = 'upon/on', سان = 'like/as'), so بر سان چشم هژبر = 'like the eye of Hezbar' (چشم هژبر is a genitive 'eye of Hezbar', with هژبر a proper name/epithet).
segments.s338-g5: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برف از زمین بادبان برکشید is a picturesque clause: 'the snow, from the ground, drew up a (sail-like) awning' — از marks origin ('from the ground'), بادبان is a metaphorical noun 'sail/awning', and برکشید is the past tense 'raised/drew up'.
segments.s338-g6: نبد is a classical negative/existential particle meaning 'there was not' or 'did not exist' placed before the following noun phrase; نیزه نامداران پدید = 'the spears of the renowned appeared' would be the positive order, so with نبد the sense is 'the spears of the champions did not appear.' پدید is the intransitive 'appear/emerge.' Note the inverted/poetic order.
segments.s338-g7: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' or 'each and every'. به برف اندرون marks the location/motion 'into/within the snow' (به + اندرون), and ماندند is past plural 'they remained' (or 'were left'). The phrase reports that each person stayed trapped in the snow.
segments.s338-g8: ندانم is the first-person negative present/past narrative 'I do not (or I do not know)'. بدان جای is an archaic fused locative ('بِدان' = 'in/at that'), here 'in that place' (جای = 'place'). چون ماندند is a subordinate interrogative/indirect question 'how they remained' (چون = 'how', ماندند = 'they remained'), so the whole clause means 'I do not know how they remained in that place.'
segments.s338-g33: به کین سیاوش is a prepositional/circumstantial phrase meaning 'by/through the vengeance of Siavash' — به here marks the cause/agent. همه کشته شد is a passive predicate: همه (the affected group) + کشته (past participle 'killed') + شد as an auxiliary, yielding 'were killed.' Note the classical/poetic use of شد as the auxiliary of the passive/result without explicit plural agreement.
segments.s338-g34: همه دوده زیر و زبر گشته شد: همه ('all/everything') is the theme; دوده is the predicate noun 'soot/blackness.' زیر و زبر is a fixed binomial 'below and above / up and down' (poetic 'everywhere'). گشته شد is the resultative construction: گشته (participle 'become/turned') + شد (auxiliary) = 'was turned / became.'
segments.s338-g35: کنون دیگر از چشم شد ناپدید places the temporal adverb (کنون 'now') and an adverbial (دیگر here functioning as 'any longer/now no more') before the verb. از چشم شدن + ناپدید is an idiomatic expression 'to disappear from sight' (چشم = sight). شد is the auxiliary forming the past/result: 'became unseen / vanished.'
segments.s338-g36: که introduces the following reported/reason clause ('for' / 'that'). دید is a past main verb 'he saw' with an implicit third-person subject carried over from context. این شگفتی is the direct object 'this wonder/marvel.' The second که is a relative/conjunction 'that,' بر من رسید uses بر + شخص to mean 'befell/arrived upon me' with رسید 'arrived' in the past sense.
segments.s338-g37: سخنهای دیرینه دستان بگفت: سخنهای دیرینه ('ancient words/tales') is the topical noun phrase, دستان here means 'the storytellers' or 'old tales' acting as agent or apposition, and بگفت is the archaic past-tense verb 'said/told.' Word order places the predicate after the nominative phrase, typical of epic diction.
segments.s338-g38: که introduces the reported content. با داد یزدان is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'with the justice of God' (با + noun). خرد باد جفت is a compact optative clause: باد is the optative particle 'may' and جفت is the predicate 'paired/matched,' so 'may wisdom be paired' (i.e. 'may wisdom be matched by God's justice'). The clause has a verbless feel with باد supplying the optative force.
segments.s338-g39: چو ... شود is a temporal subordinate clause ('when ... becomes'). از برف ('out of/from the snow') specifies the origin/condition; پیدا شود is a passive/impersonal 'become visible / appear' (پیدا = visible/found, شود = become). راه شاه = 'the king's road' (noun + possessor) follows Persian adjective/genitive order typical of the poetry.
segments.s338-g40: مگر here introduces a modal/hypothetical sense ('perhaps' / 'might it be that'); بازگردند and یابند are third-person plural subjunctive/jussive forms used to express potential or hoped-for actions ('they may return' / 'they may find'). و links the two verbs; راه is the object of یابند ('find the way'). Note the archaic use of subjunctive endings -ند for potential outcome.
segments.s338-g25: بدیشان is a dative/benefactive phrase 'for him/them' (here 'for him'), placed before the verb for emphasis; همه is an ordinary quantifier 'all/everyone'; زار and گریان form a fixed paired predicate 'zār o geryān' meaning 'to lament/waile' (zar here = 'lament/wail') and شدند is the past plural of شدن 'become' used as a copular/perfective marker, so the whole segment reads 'they all became lamenting and weeping' = 'they all wailed and wept for him.'
segments.s338-g26: بر آن آتش درد is a prepositional phrase 'by/on that fire of pain' where آن points back to the previously mentioned grief; آتش درد is a poetic nominal compound 'fire of sorrow/pain'; بریان is a past-participial/adjectival form 'roasted/burned' used predicatively; شدند again is the past plural 'became/were', so 'they were burned/consumed by that fire of sorrow.'
segments.s338-g27: همی کند is the iterative/habitual construction (همی + present/past stem) meaning 'kept doing' or 'was continually doing'; گودرز کشواد is an appositive naming the man (Goudarz, [son of] Kashvad) functioning as the subject; موی is the direct object 'hair', so the clause means 'Goudarz (Kashvad's scion) kept pulling/tearing at his hair.'
segments.s338-g28: This line continues with two coordinate actions: همی ریخت آب 'water kept pouring' where آب (here metonymically 'tears') is the thing that flows and ریخت is the sense 'to pour/flow'; و links the next clause; همی خست روی is another iterative predicate with خست (here 'become tired/wan') modifying روی 'face' — literally 'his face became weary.' The repetition of همی gives a continuous, scene-setting effect.
segments.s338-g29: همی گفت is the iterative past 'kept saying' (همی marking continuity, گفت = 'said'); گودرز is the speaker; کاین کس is a demonstrative + noun 'this/that person' used in a negative sense; ندید is the negative past 'did not see' (here 'had not seen' or 'saw no one'), so the clause means 'Goudarz kept saying that he had seen no such person.'
segments.s338-g30: که introduces a relative/comment clause 'who/that'; از تخم کاووس is a genitive-like phrase 'from the seed/offspring of Kavus' (تخم used figuratively = 'descendants'); بر من is the dative 'to me' or 'toward me'; رسید is the past 'arrived/reached', so the subordinate clause means 'who descended from Kavus came to me' (negated by the previous ندید).
segments.s338-g31: نبیره پسر داشتم is an ownership statement in past tense: نبیره (scion/descendant) + پسر (son) form a noun phrase 'a scion, a son', and داشتم is the past 'I had'. لشکری follows as an attributive adjective/noun 'military'/'of troops', so the sense is 'I had a scion/son, a man of troops (i.e. with an army).' Word order places the possessive verb after the noun phrase, typical of Persian.
segments.s338-g32: جهاندار is an epithet 'world-conquering/sovereign' applied to the son (an honorific/title); و links the following prepositional phrase بر هر سری افسری 'on every head a diadem/helmet' where بر هر سری = 'on every head' and افسری (here) means 'diadem/helmet' — together giving the image of an army with a helmet on each head.
segments.s338-g17: بگفتند is a reporting verb in the past plural ('they said'). کاین introduces the reported content as a demonstrative ('this/that') and کار is the noun 'affair/thing' being referred to. شد is the simple past 'happened/became' describing the state of that affair, and با درنگ is a prepositional phrase ('with delay') modifying how it happened. The whole segment is a reported-clause nucleus: 'they said that this matter happened with delay.'
segments.s338-g18: چنین is a discourse adverb 'thus/so' commenting on the prior clause; چند here means 'a few/several' and quantifies the duration. باشیم is the first-person plural subjunctive/optative (classical usage equivalent to 'let us be / let us stay'), so the phrase is a proposal 'let us remain.' بر + noun gives location 'on/upon' and کوه و سنگ is the coordinate location 'mountain and rock(s)'. Word order (چنین چند باشیم بر کوه و سنگ) places the manner/duration before the verb, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s338-g19: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). شاه is the subject 'the king.' شد here means 'became' (i.e., 'becomes/turns into') and combines with ناپدید to mean 'becomes vanished' or 'disappears.' از جهان is a prepositional phrase 'from the world.' The clause is a protasis expressing the hypothetical disappearance of the king.
segments.s338-g20: چو here works as a comparative/simile 'as' or 'like.' باد هوا is literal 'the wind (and) the air' where باد is 'wind' and هوا 'air' (here physical atmosphere). از میان is the prepositional 'from between/from the midst,' and بردمید is an older past-tense verb meaning 'carried off' or 'swept away.' The whole segment is a simile: 'as the wind carries the air away from between.'
segments.s338-g21: دگر before a plural noun functions as 'other' ('the other famous ones'). نامداران is used as a plural noun 'the renowned/illustrious men.' کجا is the interrogative 'where,' and رفته‌اند is the present-perfect/pluperfect sense 'have gone' or 'are gone.' The clause asks the location/status of those other nobles.
segments.s338-g22: مگر here introduces a rhetorical question/expectation ('is it not the case that / perhaps'), followed by پند 'advice' and خسرو the addressee/source ('Khosrow'). نپذرفته‌اند is a negated perfect 'they have not accepted.' Together the line questions whether the nobles have failed to accept Khosrow's counsel (rhetorical/expectant tone).
segments.s338-g23: ببودند is a past/pluperfect-type form meaning 'they were/stayed' (classical narrative past for a continuing state). یک هفته is a duration 'one week.' بر پشت کوه is a locative phrase 'on the back/slope of the mountain' (پشت here 'back/behind' as a part of the mountain). The segment states how long and where they remained.
segments.s338-g24: سر هفته is an idiom meaning 'by the end of the week' (سر here used in the temporal sense 'at/the end of'). گشتند is the verbs 'they became' (change of state). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether,' and ستوه is an adjective 'weary/upset.' The clause means that at week's end they had become completely weary.
segments.s338-g49: 'جهان را' is topicalized/marked by the object marker را (the world is being put forward as the thing described). The clause uses the fixed poetic formula 'چنین است' ('thus is/it is thus') and 'آیین و دین' is the predicate ('custom and law/religion') describing that world-topic.
segments.s338-g50: 'نماندست' is an archaic fused negative-verb form meaning 'does not remain' or 'is not kept'; 'همواره' (always) modifies this verb. Here 'در' means 'door/gate' (not the locative 'in'), and 'به گزین' ('به' + infinitive 'گزین') expresses the act/purpose 'to choose' — together the phrase says there is not always the gate/opportunity at which to choose.
segments.s338-g51: The indefinite/topic 'یکی را' ('one/person') is marked by را as the direct object. 'ز' is the poetic ablative 'from' and 'خاک سیه' ('black dust/soil') gives the origin; the verb 'برکشد' (باَلفعلِ بر- + کشد) denotes lifting up/'raising' someone (literary present/past sense 'he raises').
segments.s338-g52: Parallel structure to the previous line: 'یکی را' again marks the person affected. 'ز تخت کیان' = 'from the throne of the Kiyans/Kayan kings' (ز = from). The verb 'درکشد' (compound with در-) here means to pull/drag away (poetic use), opposite in sense to 'برکشد'.
segments.s338-g53: The correlative negatives 'نه ... نه ...' give 'neither ... nor ...'. Each branch uses the ablative 'ز' ('from/because of'): 'نه ز این شاد باشد' = 'not happy because of this' and 'نه ز آن دردمند' = 'nor sorrowful because of that.' 'باشد' is the literary copula/subjunctive form used here as the simple 'is/be'.
segments.s338-g54: 'چنین است' again is the formulaic 'thus it is'. 'رسم' ('custom/manner') is the head noun and 'سرای گزند' is a genitive/attributive phrase ('the house/abode of harm'); together the noun phrase 'رسم سرای گزند' means 'the custom/way of the house of harm' or more freely 'the pattern of harm', which the copula states is 'thus'.
segments.s338-g55: The sentence begins with the interrogative 'کجا' ('where'). 'آن یلان و کیان جهان' is a compound nominal group: 'those heroes and the Kayan kings of the world' — demonstrative 'آن' precedes the coordinated nouns; 'جهان' scopes as 'of the world' for the phrase.
segments.s338-g56: 'از اندیشه' is an ablative prepositional phrase 'from thought'; 'دل' (heart) is the direct object. The verb phrase 'دور کن' is an imperative 'make/move [it] distant' (put away), and 'تا توان' ('as far as you can') is a limit clause with 'توان' as 'ability' — so the whole means 'set your heart apart from thought as much as you are able.'
segments.s338-g9: زمانی is an adverbial temporal modifier placed before the verb meaning 'for a while' (archaic/poetic ordering). تپیدند is the past 3rd-person-plural of تپیدن ('to throb/beat'), so the clause reads literally 'for a while they throbbed'. در زیر برف is a prepositional phrase ('in/under the snow') placed after the verb to give the location; note the two-part preposition در زیر is a poetic doubling ('in beneath') meaning simply 'beneath'.
segments.s338-g10: یکی here is an indefinite 'one/a certain' used adverbially to mark 'in one place' or 'somewhere'. چاه شد کنده is an older passive/result expression: شد (become) + کنده (past participle) yields 'became dug' / 'was formed as a pit' — the verb شد converts the participle into a resulting state. هر جای ژرف follows as a locative modifier 'a deep place' or 'in the deep ground', so the whole phrase means 'a deep pit was formed in one place' (poetic compression of place and result).
segments.s338-g11: نماند is the past negative 'was not left / remained not' (negation built into the verb form). ایچ کس را is the negative indefinite object phrase 'not a single person' with را used as an object/topic marker; از ایشان ('from them') locates the group. توان here is a nominal 'power/ability' placed after the locative phrase so the clause literally reads 'no power remained from them (i.e. none of them retained ability)'.
segments.s338-g12: برآمد is a past intransitive 'emerged / came forth' (here conveying the idea of coming out or issuing forth). به introduces the goal/direction 'to/into'. فرجام شیرین is a noun phrase 'a sweet end/outcome' and روان ('soul / spirit') is postposed as the possessor/affected entity — together 'to the soul's sweet end' or more smoothly 'to a sweet fate for the soul'. Grammatically this is a goal phrase that completes the idea of 'no ability remained to emerge to a sweet end'.
segments.s338-g13: همی is an archaic/poetic continuative particle meaning 'still' or marking a continuing state. بود is the past copula 'was/were' (here 3rd-person singular); رستم is the subject. بر آن کوهسار is the locative prepositional phrase 'on that mountain-side' placed after the verb (poetic word order puts verb before subject).
segments.s338-g14: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing back to previously mentioned people; it introduces the list زال و گودرز (proper names). و is the coordinating 'and'. چندی is an adjective/adverb meaning 'some/several' that modifies سوار ('riders/horsemen'), so 'and several riders' completes the enumerated group present with Rostam.
segments.s338-g15: بدان کوه is the prepositional locative 'on that mountain' (بدان = به + آن, an archaic fused form). بودند is the past 3rd-person-plural 'they were'. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely / continuously / straight' and combines with سه روز ('three days') to mean 'for three whole days.' The phrase places duration and location: 'they were on that mountain for three full days.'
segments.s338-g16: چهارم is an adverbial/temporal ordinal 'on the fourth (day)'. چو is the conjunction 'when'. بفروخت is a past-form verb from فروختن ('to sell') but here is used poetically/archaically — contextually it conveys the coming of light or a change in the world (effectively 'when the world became bright' or 'when daylight broke'); this is a conventional poetic compression where literal 'sell' is not the intended everyday sense. گیتی فروز can be read as 'the world-brightening' (epithet) or literally 'the world' (گیتی) + فروز ('bright'), so the clause functions as 'on the fourth, when the world brightened...'.
segments.s338-g41: نشاید is an impersonal/predicative verb meaning 'it is not fitting' (classical register). بدین is the prepositional prefix ب- + دین (here meaning 'to/at this' in the phrase; the word list is slightly mis-tokenized), so بدین کوه = 'on/at this mountain'. The phrase سر بر بدن is literal word order 'head upon body' (سر = head, بر = on/upon, بدن = body); the entire segment says 'it is not fitting on this mountain [to] (have) head upon body'.
segments.s338-g42: خورش نیست is a simple negative existential clause 'there is no food/provisions here' (خورش used generically for victuals). ز ایدر introduces place 'from/here' (ز + ایدر = 'from here' / 'here'); بباید شدن is an older/modal periphrasis meaning 'one must (go/leave)'—بباید expresses necessity and شدن here functions as the verbal infinitive/auxiliary indicating the action that must take place.
segments.s338-g43: پیاده here is adverbial 'on foot' (placed before the verb for emphasis). فرستیم is past tense, 1st person plural 'we sent'. چندی is a quantifying/adverbial word 'a few' or 'for some time' modifying how many/who were sent. به راه is the directional prepositional phrase 'along/to the road'.
segments.s338-g44: بیابند is a subjunctive/present-tense form of 'to find' used here with the sense 'may/should find' (purpose/optative). روزی functions adverbially 'one day' or 'someday' (the lexical senses list did not include this sense). نشان سپاه = 'a sign/token of the army' — نشان (sign) is the direct object and سپاه (army) modifies it.
segments.s338-g45: برفتند is the simple past 3rd-person plural 'they departed/left'. زان = ز + آن 'from that' indicating origin (from that mountain). گریان is a participial/adjectival form 'weeping' and به درد is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in sorrow/pain' modifying the manner of departure.
segments.s338-g46: همی is a continuative/eminence particle meaning 'continually/still' placed before the clause. هر کسی 'each person' is the subject; از کسی یاد کرد is the idiom 'to remember someone' where یاد کردن takes an object introduced by از ('to remember/of someone'). کرد is the past auxiliary 'did/remembered' completing the verbal phrase.
segments.s338-g47: ز introduces a list of people being remembered: ز فرزند و خویشان وز دوستان = 'of/they thought of (his) sons and relatives and friends'. The token وز is a conflation of و + ز ('and' + 'from/of') in the manuscriptal/poetic spacing; in sense-mapping the exact lexical senses supplied do not match that fused token.
segments.s338-g48: و زآن = 'and of that' (زآن = 'of/from that'); شاه is 'the king'. چون سرو در بوستان is a simile 'like a cypress in the garden' (چون = like/as, سرو = cypress as a praise-image of tall beauty, در بوستان = in the garden). The whole segment completes the objects of 'remembered' from the previous clause.

sentences.s338-s1: After that they ate whatever there was, and from eating they soon went to sleep.
sentences.s338-s2: At once a wind and a cloud arose; the air turned to resemble the eye of Hezbar.
sentences.s338-s3: When the snow raised a sail from the ground, the spears of the champions did not appear.
sentences.s338-s4: One by one they remained inside the snow; I do not know how they remained in that place.
sentences.s338-s17: By Siavash's vengeance everyone was slain; everything was turned to soot, above and below.
sentences.s338-s18: Now he vanished from sight; for he saw this marvel that befell me.
sentences.s338-s19: The old tales of the storytellers said that, by the justice of God, may wisdom be paired (i.e. may wisdom be matched).
sentences.s338-s20: When the king's road becomes visible from the snow, perhaps they will return and find the way.
sentences.s338-s13: They all wailed and wept for him; they were burned by that fire of sorrow.
sentences.s338-s14: Goudarz, son of Kashvad, kept tearing at his hair; tears flowed and his face grew weary.
sentences.s338-s15: Goudarz kept saying that he had seen no one of Kavus's seed come to him.
sentences.s338-s16: I had a scion, a son — a commander of armies — and a helmet (diadem) on every head.
sentences.s338-s9: They said that this affair had happened with delay; so let us remain like this for a few days on the mountain and the rocks.
sentences.s338-s10: If the king should vanish from the world — like the wind that carries the air away from between.
sentences.s338-s11: Where have the other renowned men gone? — Have they not accepted Khosrow's counsel?
sentences.s338-s12: They stayed one week on the back of the mountain; by the end of the week they became entirely weary.
sentences.s338-s25: Such is the world's custom and law: there is not always an opportunity at the gate to choose.
sentences.s338-s26: He raises one from the black dust, he drags another from the Kayan throne.
sentences.s338-s27: Neither is he made happy by this, nor sorrowful because of that; such is the custom of the house of harm.
sentences.s338-s28: Where are those heroes and the Kayan kings of the world? Put your heart away from thought as far as you can.
sentences.s338-s5: For a while they throbbed beneath the snow; in one place a deep pit was formed.
sentences.s338-s6: Not one of them had any strength left to emerge to a sweet fate for the soul.
sentences.s338-s7: Rostam remained on that mountain-side — Zal and Goudarz and several riders (were there) as well.
sentences.s338-s8: They were on that mountain the whole three days; on the fourth, when the world brightened...
sentences.s338-s21: It is not fitting on this mountain to place a head upon a body; there is no food here, so one must leave.
sentences.s338-s22: We sent a few on foot along the road so that they might find, some day, a sign of the army.
sentences.s338-s23: They departed from that mountain weeping in grief; each person recalled someone else.
sentences.s338-s24: They remembered his sons and kinsmen and friends, and that king—like a cypress in a garden.
